2: Yom Kippur (Day of Atonement) is the annual reset of Israel's covenant relationship: the accumulated sin of the year is dealt with by the high priest's double ritual — (1) the bull's blood for his own sin, (2) one goat killed for the people's sin (the sacrificed goat), (3) one goat bearing the sins symbolically into the wilderness (the scapegoat, azazel). The two-goat ritual together communicates the complete theology of atonement: blood-payment (the dead goat) + removal (the scapegoat). Christ fulfills both aspects: his blood makes propitiation and his bearing of sin removes it ('as far as the east is from the west, so far does he remove our transgressions from us', Ps 103:12).
1: The Yom Kippur legislation is grounded in the death of Nadab and Abihu (Lev 10:1-2), who entered YHWH's presence without authorization. The historical reference (aḥarei mot shney bnei Aharon, 'after the death of the two sons of Aaron') sets the chapter in the aftermath of a catastrophic priestly failure. Yom Kippur is the divinely-given solution: an annual, authorized, highly regulated pathway into the divine presence, structured to prevent the death that comes from unauthorized approach.
3: bezot yavo Aharon el-haqqodesh: 'In this way Aaron shall enter the holy place.' The emphatic demonstrative bezot (in this / by this) marks the entry protocol as the sole legitimate pathway. The two animals: par ben-baqar (young bull) for the sin offering (ḥatta't) for Aaron's own sin, and a ram for the burnt offering (olah). The priest must address his own impurity before mediating for the people — a point Hebrews exploits to contrast Christ, who had no sin of his own (Heb 7:27).
4: ketonet-bad qodesh yilbash: 'He shall put on the holy linen tunic.' On Yom Kippur the high priest exchanges his normal elaborate golden vestments (Exod 28) for simple white linen — bad (linen) repeated four times in this verse. The linen is white (lavan), associated with purity. The high priest enters the holy of holies not in his symbols of status but in symbols of purity — a reversal of normal priestly display. The four linen garments all belong to YHWH (bigdei qodesh, holy garments).
5: From the congregation: two male goats (se'irei izzim) for the sin offering and one ram for the burnt offering. The two goats are a single sin offering taken together — they function as a paired unit representing the two aspects of atonement: blood-payment (the slaughtered goat) and removal (the scapegoat). One offering, two mechanisms. This two-part structure will be explained through vv. 15-22.
6: vehiqriv Aharon et-par haḥatta't asher-lo vekhipper ba'ado uvead beito: 'Aaron shall offer the bull of his sin offering and make atonement for himself and his household.' The sequential structure requires the priest's own atonement first — he cannot mediate for others until he himself has been cleansed. Bead beito (for his household) includes his wife and all dependent family members. The necessity of priestly self-atonement is the main contrast point of Heb 7:27: 'He has no need, like those high priests, to offer sacrifices daily, first for his own sins and then for those of the people.'
7: The two goats are presented together at the sanctuary entrance. The paired presentation before YHWH is necessary before lot-casting — both goats belong to the ritual simultaneously; they are not yet differentiated. The structural parallel with the two birds in the leper's cleansing (Lev 14:4-7) is noted by later interpreters: one is killed, one released; both together represent cleansing.
8: venatan Aharon al-shnei hase'irim goralot: 'Aaron shall cast lots over the two goats.' Goralot (lots) — the method is not described precisely; later tradition (Mishnah Yoma) used two inscribed gold tablets. The critical term: azazel. This is the most debated term in Leviticus. Three main interpretations: (1) a place name ('land of removal/desolation'); (2) a compound meaning 'goat of removal' or 'complete removal' (ez-azal); (3) a supernatural entity — a wilderness demon or fallen angel to whom the goat is dispatched (supported by 1 Enoch, Dead Sea Scrolls, and the parallel with goat-demons in 17:7). The MKT renders azazel as a proper name, leaving the ambiguity intact.
9: The goat assigned to YHWH by lot becomes the sin offering — it is slaughtered, its blood brought inside the veil. This is the propitiatory goat: its blood atones in the sanctuary, cleansing the holy place from accumulated Israelite impurity. The lot procedure ensures that neither goat is pre-selected by human choice — the assignment is YHWH's own determination.
10: veha-sa'ir asher ala alav hagoral la'aza'zel ya'amod-ḥai lifnei YHWH lekapper alav: 'The goat on which the lot for Azazel fell shall be presented alive before YHWH to make atonement over it.' The live goat's function is also atonement (kipper) — but its mechanism is different: not blood-sprinkling but confession and removal. It is first presented before YHWH (legitimizing the ritual), then used for confession, then released. The word ḥai (alive) emphasizes that this goat does not die in the ritual; its death outside the camp (or at the cliff, per later tradition) is incidental to its function.
11: Aaron slaughters his bull first — before the people's goat — demonstrating the sequential logic: self-atonement must precede intercession. Vekhipper ba'ado uvead beito (and he shall make atonement for himself and his household) is repeated from v. 6, reinforcing the priority of priestly cleansing. The blood of Aaron's bull will be used inside the holy of holies (v. 14).
12: veleqaḥ melo-hammaḥtah gaḥalei-esh me'al hamizbeaḥ: 'He shall take a censer full of burning coals from the altar.' The incense censer is the high priest's shield for entering the holy of holies: he carries coals from the bronze altar and two handfuls of finely ground fragrant incense (qetoret sammim daqah). The incense cloud (anan heqetoret) will cover the kapporet, protecting the priest from seeing YHWH directly. The smoke is protective, not merely ceremonial — velo yamut (and he shall not die) is the goal.
13: venatan et-haqetoret al-ha'esh lifnei YHWH vekissa anan-haqetoret et-hakapporet: 'He shall put the incense on the fire before YHWH so that the cloud of incense covers the atonement cover.' The incense cloud functions as a veil between the priest's sight and the divine presence localized at the kapporet. The theology: YHWH's presence is genuinely located here, but direct exposure is lethal. The incense mediates access. Later tradition (Mishnah Yoma 5:1) preserves Pharisaic debate over whether the incense should be lit before or after entering the veil — a halakhic dispute reflecting the verse's precise sequence.
14: velakaḥ middam hapar vehizza beetzba'o al-pnei hakapporet qedmah: 'He shall take some of the bull's blood and sprinkle it with his finger on the front of the atonement cover eastward.' The blood is applied to the kapporet (the divine throne) directly. The direction: qedmah (eastward/on the east face) — once on the face of the kapporet itself, then seven times before it on the floor. Seven is the completion number (Gen 1). The blood-sprinkling on and before the kapporet is the central act of the entire Yom Kippur ritual — it is where atonement happens at its most concentrated point.
15: The same blood-sprinkling procedure is repeated with the people's goat blood inside the holy of holies. Aaron sprinkles bull blood (for his own sin) and goat blood (for the people's sin) on and before the kapporet in the same sequence. The doubled application — priest's blood then people's blood — makes Yom Kippur atonement comprehensive: both priesthood and congregation are cleansed from sin accumulated during the year.
16: vekhipper al-haqqodesh mitum'ot bnei Yisrael umippish'eihem lekhol-ḥatotam: 'He shall make atonement for the holy place because of the uncleannesses of the Israelites and because of their transgressions, for all their sins.' Three categories are distinguished: tumot (ritual uncleannesses — the accumulated impurity from birth, death, bodily flows), psha'im (willful transgressions/rebellions), ḥatot (sins generally). The holy place itself has been contaminated by Israel's sins even though they never entered it — the sanctuary absorbs impurity from the camp. Yom Kippur cleanses the sanctuary as much as the people.
17: vekhol-adam lo-yihyeh be'ohel mo'ed bevo'o lekapper baqqodesh: 'No person shall be in the tent of meeting when he enters to make atonement in the holy place.' Total isolation of the high priest during the inner-sanctuary work. This is the moment of greatest solemnity — the entire community waits outside while the single mediator stands before YHWH. The exclusion of 'all persons' (kol-adam) reinforces the uniqueness of the high-priestly role. The NT will use this structure: Christ enters the true holy of holies alone, as the sole mediator (1 Tim 2:5; Heb 9:7, 11-12).
18: veyatsa el-hamizbeaḥ asher lifnei YHWH vekhipper alav: 'He shall come out to the altar that is before YHWH and make atonement for it.' Having cleansed the inner sanctuary, the high priest moves outward: first the altar of incense (implied), then the bronze altar. The outward movement is spatially significant — atonement radiates from the most holy place outward through the entire sanctuary complex. Blood from both animals is mixed and applied to the four horns of the altar.
19: vehizza alav min-hadam beetzba'o sheva pe'amim: 'He shall sprinkle some of the blood on it with his finger seven times.' The sevenfold sprinkling (also at v. 14) is the completion number — seven applications ensure comprehensive cleansing. Veteherhu veqiddesho mitum'ot bnei Yisrael (cleansing it and consecrating it from the uncleannesses of the Israelites): the altar's dual state change — from impure to clean, from ordinary to consecrated — accomplished by blood application.
20: The transition from sanctuary-cleansing to scapegoat: the live goat is now summoned. The sequencing is deliberate — the sanctuary must be cleansed first; only then is the confession ritual performed over the live goat. The two mechanisms address different aspects: the blood deals with impurity in the sanctuary; the scapegoat deals with iniquity symbolically removed from the community.
21: vesomakh Aharon et-shetei yadav al-rosh hasa'ir haḥai vehitvaddah alav: 'Aaron shall lay both his hands on the head of the live goat and confess over it all the iniquities of the Israelites.' The semikah here uses both hands (shetei yadav) — the intensive form, reserving full weight and identification for this act. The verbal confession (viddui) spoken over the goat is the mechanism of transfer: the named iniquities of Israel are laid onto the goat. Kol-avonot bnei Yisrael (all the iniquities of the Israelites) + kol-pisha'eihem lekhol-ḥatotam (all their transgressions, all their sins) — the triple formula covers everything.
22: venasa hasa'ir alav et-kol-avonotam el-erets gezerah: 'The goat shall carry all their iniquities to a land cut off.' Nasa (to carry/bear) is the same verb used for bearing sin throughout the OT and in Isa 53:12 (vehu ḥet-rabbim nasa, 'he bore the sins of many'). Erets gezerah (land cut off / desolate/isolated land) — the goat goes to a place of complete separation. The removal is spatial and irreversible: the community's sins go away, carried by a living being, to a place from which there is no return.
23: Aaron re-enters the tent of meeting to remove the Yom Kippur linen garments and leave them inside — they are too holy for ordinary use and cannot re-enter the camp. He then bathes and puts on his regular high-priestly vestments before offering the burnt offerings. The change of garments marks the transition from the extraordinary (holy of holies entry) back to the ordinary high-priestly ministry.
24: Aaron bathes in the holy place, dresses in his regular garments, and goes out to offer his own and the people's burnt offerings (olot). The burnt offerings (one ram each for Aaron and the congregation) complete the Yom Kippur complex — the sin offerings cleanse; the burnt offerings restore the relationship and express total dedication. The sequence: cleansing → restoration.
25: The fat (ḥelev) of the sin-offering animals is burned on the altar — the portion belonging to YHWH. Even on Yom Kippur, the standard rule that fat belongs to YHWH is maintained (Lev 3:16-17). The burning of fat is a reiaḥ niḥoaḥ (pleasing aroma) offering even when the main blood-work has been unusual.
26: The man who led the scapegoat away (meshalaḥ hasa'ir la'aza'zel) becomes ritually impure through the act — he has been the vehicle by which Israel's sins were conveyed outside the camp. He must wash his clothes and bathe before returning. The impurity is not moral guilt but ritual contamination acquired by handling the sin-laden goat.
27: The carcasses of the bull and the goat (whose blood was brought into the holy place) must be taken outside the camp and burned — skin, flesh, and dung. The fire outside the camp (miḥuts lammaḥaneh) is the disposal mechanism for the most holy sin-offering animals whose blood entered the inner sanctuary. This rule is cited by Heb 13:11-12 as the type of Christ suffering 'outside the gate.'
28: The man who burns the carcasses also becomes unclean and must wash and bathe. Three people acquire impurity through Yom Kippur rituals: (1) the scapegoat handler (v. 26), (2) the carcass-burner (v. 28), and (3) implicitly Aaron himself requires bathing (v. 24). The entire ritual generates contagion in the participants — the mediation of sin is not cost-free even for those facilitating atonement.
29: vehayetah lachem leḥuqqat olam baḥodesh hashvei'i be'asor laḥodesh te'annu et-nafshoteikhem: 'This shall be a statute forever for you: on the tenth day of the seventh month you shall practice self-denial.' Inui nefesh (affliction/self-denial of soul) is the biblical prescription for Yom Kippur fasting — the noun innui (affliction) from the verb anah (to afflict/humble). Rabbinic tradition identified five deprivations: no eating, no drinking, no bathing, no anointing, no marital relations. Leḥuqqat olam (a statute forever) marks this as a permanent covenant obligation.
30: ki vayom hazeh yekhapper aleikhem letaher etkhem mikol ḥatoteikhem lifnei YHWH titharu: 'For on this day atonement shall be made for you to cleanse you; from all your sins before YHWH you shall be clean.' The climactic statement of Yom Kippur's purpose: comprehensive cleansing (taher) from all sins. Lifnei YHWH (before YHWH) — the cleansing is not merely social or psychological but covenantal and relational, occurring in YHWH's presence. The passive titharu (you shall be clean) emphasizes that cleansing is something done to Israel, not accomplished by Israel's effort.
31: Shabbat Shabbaton hi lachem: 'It is a Shabbat of complete rest for you.' Shabbat Shabbaton is the superlative construction (Sabbath of Sabbaths = the ultimate/complete Sabbath) — the highest intensity of the Sabbath principle. Yom Kippur is the only day in the Torah calendar given this designation, shared only with the seventh-year land sabbath (Lev 25:4) and one other observance. The phrase combines the annual atonement calendar with the weekly rest rhythm, marking it as a peak moment in Israel's entire temporal structure.
32: The law applies to any legitimately anointed and ordained high priest, not only to Aaron personally — asher-yimshaḥ oto vaasher yemale yadav (who is anointed and whose hand is filled) = the full ordination formula. This forward-looking clause establishes Yom Kippur as an ongoing priestly institution, not a one-time event tied to Aaron's personal ministry.
33: The comprehensive scope of Yom Kippur atonement: the holy sanctuary (miqdash haqqodesh), the tent of meeting, the altar, the priests, and all the congregation of Israel (kol-am haqqahal). Atonement radiates outward from the holy of holies through every layer of the sanctuary and community. No part of Israel's covenant structure is excluded from the annual cleansing.
34: lekhapper al-bnei Yisrael mikol-ḥatotam aḥat bashanah: 'to make atonement for Israel from all their sins once a year.' Aḥat bashanah (once a year) — the limitation is the key theological datum exploited by Hebrews: the annual repetition proves that Yom Kippur could not achieve permanent removal of sin (Heb 10:1-3). If the sacrifice had accomplished its goal, the worshippers would have been cleansed once for all and the annual ritual would have been unnecessary. The repetition is the system's built-in indicator that it was provisional, designed to point forward to a once-for-all (ephapax) sacrifice.
